other_observers: seen with Nick Komar, Bill Prather, Scott Rashid, Lisa Edwards, Brandon Percival, and others; first found by Bill Prather
description: the bird was a medium sized shorebird, noticeably smaller than nearby American Avocets, but noticeably larger than nearby Lesser Yellowlegs. The bird had a very long bill, fairly thick at the base and tapering slightly to a blunt tip. The bill had a slight but obvious upturn. The basal half of the bill was a reddish-orange color, the distal half was black. The legs were black.  The overall color of the head was gray, contrasting with the darker back and underparts. The crown was darker gray, with black streaks, and the bird also had a darker gray (though paler than the crown) eye-line, which contrasted with the pale gray supercilium, face, and upper neck. The upper neck also had some faint darker gray streaks, darker towards the lower neck. The back of the bird was very dark gray, with small, pale buffy dots. The underparts were a dark brick red, with faint dark gray streaks towards the upper neck and faint black bars on the flanks. The undertail coverts were white, with strong black bars.  The primaries, when folded, came to a point just beyond the tip of the tail. In flight, the flight feathers were dark gray, and there was a faint white stripe at the base of the flight feathers. The wing linings were black, contrasting with the gray color of the flight feathers underneath (in the photo I have of the wing lifted the flight feathers appear white, which is an artifact of the bright sunlight on the feathers). The tail was white at the basal half and had a bold black patch at the distal half.Behaviors: The bird was observed feeding and loafing at the edge of the water. It would feed by rapidly walking around looking down, and occasionally dipping its bill quickly into the water. The only times I saw it fly was when it was flushed by a raptor, and it would quickly return to the area it had just left.Call: none heardPlumage: alternate
photographer: Photographer: Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer
